title: Why start here
paras: Why start here? Because almost every mistake in English planning — the expensive ones and the merely embarrassing ones — comes from starting in the wrong place. People reach for the policy, the design, the neighbours, the appeal statistics, before they've settled the three questions that come first — the questions that decide everything after. This essay is about those three questions, taken in strict order. And the order is the whole point.

So what are the three questions? First: is the thing you're proposing development at all? If it isn't, the planning system has nothing to say about it, and the conversation is over before it begins. Second: if it is development, where does permission for it come from? There are only two possible sources, and knowing which one applies changes everything you do next. And third: once permission exists, has development lawfully begun? A permission that's never lawfully implemented is worth nothing — and a great deal of value is destroyed every year by people who thought they'd started when they hadn't.

The order matters because each question depends on the one before it. You can't sensibly ask where permission comes from until you know whether you need permission at all. You can't ask whether development has begun until you know that a valid permission exists to begin. Skip a step, or take them out of sequence, and you build your analysis on air. The habit this essay is arguing for is dull and unglamorous — and it's the single most valuable habit in the field. Take the questions in order, every time, and don't move to the next until the current one is answered.

title: The starting point: section 55
paras: Now, the starting point: section 55. Everything begins with one word and one section. The word is development, and the section is section 55 of the Town and Country Planning Act 1990. If you learn one piece of statute by heart, learn this one. Section 55, subsection 1, says this.

Subject to the following provisions of this section, in this Act, except where the context otherwise requires, development means the carrying out of building, engineering, mining or other operations in, on, over or under land, or the making of any material change in the use of any buildings or other land.

Read it slowly, because it has a clear shape. There are two limbs, joined by the word or. The first limb is operations — building, engineering, mining or other operations, carried out in, on, over or under land. That's the physical stuff: the digging and building and altering of the land itself. The second limb is a material change in the use of buildings or land. And that isn't about physical works at all. It's about what the land is used for, and whether that use has changed in a way that matters. The word material here simply means significant enough to count in planning terms, as opposed to trivial. We'll come back to it.

Section 55 is the definition. The gateway is section 57, subsection 1, which provides that planning permission is required for the carrying out of any development of land. Put the two together and you have the founding rule of the whole system. Planning permission is required for development — and it's required only for development. If what you're proposing doesn't fall within the section 55 definition, then it isn't development, section 57 isn't engaged, and planning control has nothing to say. The system doesn't reach it. That's why the first question is not, will I get permission — it's, do I need permission at all. And it's why the section 55 definition is the door through which every planning matter must pass.

title: The first limb: operational development
paras: Take the two limbs in turn, starting with the first: operational development. That's the shorthand practitioners use for the first limb — building, engineering, mining or other operations. The Act gives some help on what building operations means. Section 55, subsection 1A, provides that building operations includes the demolition of buildings, rebuilding, structural alterations of or additions to buildings, and — in a nicely open-ended phrase — other operations normally undertaken by a person carrying on business as a builder. That last phrase is deliberately broad. If it's the kind of thing a builder does, it's likely to be a building operation.

Engineering operations covers works of the kind an engineer would carry out: the reprofiling of land, the forming of accesses and hardstandings, the construction of ponds and bunds, substantial earthworks. Mining operations covers the winning of minerals. And other operations is the catch-all — though the courts have kept it tethered to the same idea of a physical operation on the land, rather than letting it run loose.

When is a structure a building? That's the recurring practical question — whether a given object is a building at all. Because if it is, putting it up is operational development that needs permission. And if it isn't, it may escape the definition entirely. The law approaches this as a matter of fact and degree — and that's a phrase you'll meet again and again. It means there's no bright-line rule; the answer depends on weighing the particular facts of the particular case. Three factors do most of the work. Size — is the thing substantial? Permanence — is it there for a limited period, or indefinitely? And physical attachment — is it fixed to the ground, or merely resting on it? A large, permanent, fixed structure is a building. A small, temporary, free-standing object usually is not.

The other side of the same coin is the chattel. A chattel is simply a movable object that isn't part of the land — a thing you could pick up and take away. A garden bench, a portable cabin, a shipping container dropped on the ground — all of these can be chattels rather than buildings, depending on those same factors of size, permanence and attachment. The classic borderline case is the caravan, and mobile structures generally, which sit awkwardly between chattel and building, and have generated decades of argument. Caravans are a special study in their own right, with their own statutory tests of what counts as a caravan and how many can go where — this essay does no more than flag that they're their own subject. The point to take away is this. The building or chattel question is one of fact and degree, judged on size, permanence and attachment. And it decides whether the first limb is engaged at all.

There's one more feature of operations, and it seems abstract now, but it matters a great deal later: operations happen at a moment, uses continue. An operation is a completed act. You dig the trench, you pour the concrete, you raise the wall — and at some identifiable moment, the operation is done. A use, by contrast, is a state of affairs that continues over time. Land used as a scrapyard is being used as a scrapyard every day that it goes on. This difference — between an act completed at a moment, and a use that continues — runs right through the system. And it's the reason, as we'll see, that the rules on how long the authority has to take enforcement action treat operations and uses differently. Hold the distinction in mind.

title: The second limb: material change of use
paras: The second limb — material change of use — is subtler, and it's where the real judgement lies. A material change in the use of land is development even where not a single brick is moved. The question is whether the use has changed, and whether that change is material — meaning significant in planning terms, rather than trivial. Materiality is judged as a matter of fact and degree, in planning terms. The test isn't whether the owner has done something different. It's whether the change is one that matters for the use and development of land — the kind of thing the planning system exists to regulate. A change that makes no real planning difference isn't material, however much it may matter to the people involved.

Start with the planning unit. You can't judge whether a use has changed without first deciding what area of land you're looking at. That area is the planning unit: the parcel of land that's considered as a whole when deciding what its use is. Getting the planning unit right is half the battle, because drawing it too large or too small distorts everything that follows. Draw it too large — taking in land that really functions separately — and a significant change on one part gets lost as a small proportion of the whole. Draw it too small — carving out a corner that really works as part of a bigger whole — and an ordinary incident of the main use looks like a brand new use. The normal starting point is the unit of occupation: the area in one person's control, used for one main purpose. But where genuinely separate activities go on in physically distinct parts of a site, each may form its own planning unit. Identifying the unit is a practical judgement, and it's the first move in any change of use analysis — not an afterthought.

Next, primary and ancillary uses. Within a planning unit there's usually one primary use — the main thing the land is for — and there may be one or more ancillary uses. Ancillary means secondary: a use that serves the main use rather than standing on its own. The office within a factory. The store within a shop. The home office within a house. An ancillary use doesn't need its own permission, because it's part and parcel of the primary use. The danger — and it's a common one — is the ancillary use that grows. What starts as a bit of storage ancillary to a shop expands until the storage is the real business. What starts as a hobby in a domestic garage becomes a commercial vehicle repair operation. At some point of fact and degree, the ancillary activity stops serving the main use and becomes a primary use in its own right — and that is a material change of use requiring permission, even though nothing was ever started in a formal sense. Watching for the ancillary use that has quietly become primary is one of the more useful things a planner does.

Then there's intensification, mixed uses, and houses. Some changes are changes of degree rather than kind. Intensification — doing much more of the same thing — can amount to a material change of use, if the intensification is great enough to change the character of the use in planning terms. Though the courts are cautious here, and a mere increase in activity is not automatically development. A mixed use — where two or more uses go on together, without either being merely ancillary to the other — is itself a distinct use. So moving from a single use to a mixed use, or altering the balance of a mixed use, can be a material change. And housing throws up two frequent cases. Turning a single dwelling into a house in multiple occupation, where unrelated people share facilities, may or may not be a material change. It depends on the scale and character of what's proposed, and the size of the house — and this is genuinely a fact and degree question, rather than a fixed rule.

There is, however, one change the Act puts beyond argument. Section 55, subsection 3 a, declares that the use as two or more separate dwellinghouses of any building previously used as a single dwellinghouse involves a material change in the use of the building, and of each part of it which is so used. In plain terms: splitting one house into two or more separate homes is always a material change of use. It doesn't matter how small the works — or whether there are any works at all. Subdivision of a dwelling into separate dwellings is development, full stop. And it always needs permission, unless permission comes from somewhere automatic.

title: What is not development: section 55(2)
paras: Now for what is not development: section 55, subsection 2. Having defined development broadly, the Act then carves out a list of things that are declared not to be development, so that planning control doesn't reach them. These sit in section 55, subsection 2, and four of them earn a mention here.

First, internal and non-external works. Works of maintenance, improvement or other alteration that affect only the interior of a building, or that don't materially affect its external appearance, are not development. You can remodel the inside of your building freely, so far as planning is concerned. Other regimes — such as building regulations and, for listed buildings, listed building consent — are another matter. It's the external effect that engages planning control.

Second, incidental uses within the curtilage of a dwellinghouse. The use of buildings or land within the curtilage of a house, for a purpose incidental to the enjoyment of the dwellinghouse as such, is not development. Curtilage means the land immediately around a house that goes with it and serves it — in ordinary terms, the garden and yard that form part of the property. And incidental means the ordinary domestic incidents of living there: keeping a greenhouse, storing the car, a home gym. But the limit is important. Incidental doesn't stretch to a full commercial business. A use that isn't really domestic at all — running a haulage yard from the back garden — falls outside the exclusion, and can be a material change of use.

Third, agriculture and forestry. The use of land for agriculture or forestry, and the use of any building occupied together with that land for those purposes, is not development. This is the exclusion most often misread, so listen carefully: it is the use that's excluded, not the building work. Putting up an agricultural building is an operation — operational development — which may be permitted development under the Order, but it is not taken outside the definition by section 55, subsection 2. The farmer who erects a large barn is carrying out development. It's only the agricultural use of the land that section 55, subsection 2, declares not to be development.

And fourth, changes within the same use class. Where a use falls within a class specified in the Use Classes Order, changing to another use within the same class is not development. The Use Classes Order groups uses into classes and, in effect, says that shuffling around within a class doesn't count as a material change. This is one of the two ways the system takes changes of use outside planning control — and it works hand in glove with the Order that grants permission for certain changes between classes.

The lesson of section 55, subsection 2, is that the broad definition in section 55, subsection 1, is not the last word. Some things that look like development are declared not to be. And a competent first-principles analysis checks the exclusions before concluding that permission is needed at all.

title: The cascade
paras: And so to the cascade. We can now state the discipline this essay is really about — the spine that holds everything together. Faced with any proposal, take these three steps, in this order, and don't move on until the current step is answered.

Step one: is it development under section 55? Apply the definition. Is it an operation, or a material change of use? And does any section 55, subsection 2, exclusion take it out? If it's not development, stop. Planning control is not engaged, and no permission is needed.

Step two: if it is development, does the General Permitted Development Order already grant permission for it? There are only two sources of planning permission: an automatic grant by the Order, or an express grant on an application. Look first to the Order — because if the proposal is permitted development, permission already exists and no application is required.

Step three: if the Order doesn't grant it, an express application is required. Section 57 admits no third way. Development that isn't permitted by the Order needs planning permission on an application to the local planning authority. There is no category of development that somehow needs no permission from either source.

That's the cascade — and it's astonishing how often it's ignored. People argue about the merits of a scheme before establishing that it's development. They assume an application is needed without checking the Order. Or they assume the Order covers them without checking whether it's development in the first place. Taking the three steps in sequence, in that order, on every matter, is the most valuable working habit in planning. It isn't clever. It's just disciplined — and discipline at first principles saves more money than brilliance in the refinements.

title: Permitted development is planning permission
paras: Permitted development is planning permission. Now for the misconception that causes more trouble than any other — and it deserves its own section, because so many people, including some who should know better, get it wrong. The belief is that permitted development doesn't need planning permission. It's false. And understanding why it's false unlocks the whole of permitted development.

The truth is that all development needs planning permission, without exception, by section 57. What the Town and Country Planning, General Permitted Development, England, Order 2015 — the G-P-D-O — does, is grant that permission in advance, automatically, for the classes of development it describes. Article 3, paragraph 1, of the Order provides that planning permission is granted for the classes of development set out in Schedule 2. So permitted development is not development that escapes the need for permission. It's development for which permission has already been granted — by the Order, rather than by the council. The permission is real. It's a planning permission, every bit as much as one granted on an application. It simply came from a different source.
Once you see permitted development as a grant of permission, three consequences follow — and a planner must hold onto all three.

First: limitations define the grant. Each Class in Schedule 2 grants permission up to defined limits — dimensions, distances, positions, volumes, and various exclusions. Those limitations are not planning policy to be weighed. They are the boundary of the grant itself. If your development stays within them, permission attaches to it. If it exceeds a limitation, by any margin, then the grant doesn't apply — and, this is the part people miss, there is no permission for the whole development, not merely for the excess. A rear extension half a metre too long is not mostly permitted, with a small overhang to sort out. It's an extension for which the Order gives no permission at all — and which is therefore wholly unauthorised, unless an application is made. Exceeding a permitted development limit isn't a minor problem. It's a total failure of the grant.

Second: conditions attach to the grant. Because permitted development is a permission, it can carry conditions — and many Classes do. Some conditions are simple: materials must match, for example. Others require prior approval, and that needs explaining properly, because it's so often muddled. Prior approval is a condition of a permission that already exists. The Order has already granted the permission. The prior approval condition says that before the development begins, the developer must ask the authority to approve certain specified matters — and only those matters. The authority is not deciding whether to grant permission, because permission is already granted. It's policing a closed list of issues — such as transport, contamination, flooding or design, depending on the Class — and it cannot refuse on grounds outside that list. In many Classes there's a further twist. If the authority fails to determine the prior approval request within the statutory period, approval is deemed to be given by default, and the developer may proceed. Prior approval, then, is a limited check on a permission that already exists — not a fresh permission decision.

And third: Article 4 directions withdraw the grant. Because the Order grants the permission, the grant can be taken away. An Article 4 direction, made by the local planning authority, withdraws permitted development rights for particular development in a particular area — commonly a conservation area, or a location under development pressure. The effect is not to make the development unlawful in principle. It's to remove the automatic grant, so that the development that would have been permitted now needs an express application in the ordinary way. The direction returns the development to the application route. It doesn't forbid it.

title: The trap inside article 3: habitats
paras: Now, the trap inside article 3: habitats. There's a trap sitting inside the grant, and it's missed even by professionals, because it lives in a different piece of legislation. The grant of permission in article 3, paragraph 1, of the Order is not unconditional. It's expressly made subject to the provisions of the Order itself — and, importantly, to regulations 75 to 78 of the Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017. Those regulations bite where otherwise-permitted development is likely to have a significant effect on a European site — the protected habitats sites, that's the Special Areas of Conservation and the Special Protection Areas, which carry the highest level of nature protection.

Where that risk exists, the permitted development must not begin until the developer has applied to the local planning authority and received its written approval. And that approval can be given only after the authority has carried out an appropriate assessment — a formal assessment of the effect on the protected site — in consultation with Natural England, the government's nature adviser. This is the Regulation 77 application that authorities in affected areas will be familiar with. Until that approval is given, the Order's grant is switched off. Development begun without it is unauthorised — exactly as if the Order had never applied to it at all. The automatic permission you thought you had was never available.

The reason this matters so much in practice is the overlap. The development that leans most heavily on permitted development rights — agricultural buildings, barn conversions to dwellings, changes of use in the countryside — tends to happen in exactly the rural areas where European sites and their catchments are found. A farmer relying on an agricultural Class, or a landowner converting a barn, may be squarely within the Order's limits and still be caught by the habitats regulations. And neither the Order nor the ordinary permitted development guidance shouts about it. Anyone advising on permitted development near a protected site, or in a nutrient-sensitive catchment, must check the habitats position before telling a client they can begin. Because a confident, it's permitted development, crack on, is worthless if regulation 75 has quietly withdrawn the grant.

title: Knowing for certain: lawful development certificates
paras: So how do you know for certain? The answer is the lawful development certificate. If permitted development is a permission you can't see — because no council ever issued a decision notice — how do you prove you have it? And how do you prove that an existing use or building has become lawful with the passage of time? The Act provides a way to obtain certainty, the lawful development certificate, and it comes in two directions.

Section 192 is forward-looking. It lets you ask the authority to certify that a proposed use or operation would be lawful if carried out — typically because it's permitted development, or because it isn't development at all. You get your certainty before you act. Section 191 is backward-looking. It lets you ask the authority to certify that an existing use, or existing operations, or something done in breach of a condition, is lawful now — typically because it's permitted development, or because the time for enforcement has passed. One certificate looks ahead to a proposal. The other looks back at what has already happened.

Three points about certificates are worth fixing in the mind. First, the test is legal, not planning merit. The authority isn't deciding whether the development is acceptable, desirable or well designed. It's deciding one question only: is it lawful? A hideous but lawful thing gets a certificate. A beautiful but unlawful thing does not. Second, the burden of proof is on the applicant, on the balance of probabilities — meaning the applicant must show it's more likely than not that the facts and the law support lawfulness. And third, certificates deal in evidence, and evidence is where they're won and lost. Contemporaneous documents — things created at the time, such as dated invoices, photographs, utility bills, tenancy agreements and statutory declarations — carry far more weight than a recollection offered years later. A retrospective statement that it's been like this since about 2010 is weak. A folder of dated paperwork proving it is strong. Build the evidence base as you go — not when the dispute arrives.

title: Commencement: section 56
paras: Commencement: section 56. Now for the third foundational question — the one people forget until it's too late. A planning permission doesn't last forever. The standard permission must be begun within three years of the date it's granted — some permissions carry a different period, but three years is the norm — and if it isn't lawfully begun in time, it lapses. A lapsed permission is dead. The value it represented, sometimes very large value, is simply gone, and can't be recovered except by applying again, in a policy world that may have moved against you. Knowing exactly what it takes to begin a development, in law, is therefore not a technicality. It's the difference between a live asset and a worthless piece of paper.

Section 56 answers the question. Development is taken to begin, in the words of section 56, subsection 2, on the earliest date on which any material operation comprised in the development begins to be carried out. And a material operation is defined in section 56, subsection 4. The list runs as follows.

Paragraph a: any work of construction in the course of the erection of a building. Paragraph a a: any work of demolition of a building. Paragraph b: the digging of a trench which is to contain the foundations, or part of the foundations, of a building. Paragraph c: the laying of any underground main or pipe to the foundations, or part of the foundations, of a building — or to any such trench as is mentioned in paragraph b. Paragraph d: any operation in the course of laying out or constructing a road, or part of a road. And paragraph e: any change in the use of any land which constitutes material development.

The list is generous, and that's not a scandal to be sniffed at. A modest but genuine start — digging a foundation trench, forming part of the site access — is enough in law to implement a permission and keep it alive indefinitely thereafter. This is technical commencement, and there's nothing improper about it. A developer who isn't yet ready to build out in full, but doesn't want to lose the permission, may lawfully carry out a real material operation to begin the development and preserve it. The key words are real and genuine. A token gesture that isn't actually one of the listed operations, or that is a sham, won't do. But an honest material operation, properly carried out, will.

And here is the trap: the Whitley principle. It destroys permissions every year. It's not enough that the works you carry out are on the list. They must also be lawful — which means carried out in accordance with the permission, including its conditions. Many permissions carry conditions precedent, also called pre-commencement conditions: a condition that must be satisfied before any development begins. For example, no development shall take place until a scheme for X has been approved in writing. If you carry out your material operation before discharging such a condition, then your works are in breach of the condition. And the principle in Whitley and Sons against the Secretary of State for Wales, decided in 1992, says that works carried out in breach of a condition are not carried out under the permission — and so may commence nothing. The clock keeps running, the three years expire, and the permission lapses, even though the developer sincerely believed they had started. The courts have since refined the Whitley principle at the edges, and there are situations in which a breach doesn't prove fatal. But no sensible person plans on the refinements. They are lifeboats, not a route.

The practical rule that follows is simple, and it should be treated as absolute. Before you carry out any material operation to implement a permission, do three things. Discharge every pre-commencement condition first. Get the authority's approvals in writing. And record the implementing works with dated photographs and other evidence, so that if the point is ever disputed, you can prove what you did and when. Do those three things, and commencement is secure. Skip them, and you're gambling the whole permission on a principle designed to catch exactly your kind of shortcut.

After commencement, two further consequences are worth stating: there's no going back, and there's the levy. First, a planning permission, once lawfully begun, cannot be abandoned. The House of Lords held in Pioneer Aggregates U-K Limited against the Secretary of State for the Environment, in 1985, that there is no doctrine by which a landowner can simply give up an implemented permission. It runs with the land, and it endures. You can't make a permission disappear by changing your mind about it. Second — and this catches people hard — commencement is the moment at which Community Infrastructure Levy liability crystallises, where the levy applies. Any reliefs or exemptions from the levy, and there are valuable ones, must be claimed and formally confirmed before development begins. And in most cases, a commencement notice must be served first. Start on site before the paperwork is in order, and the relief is lost irretrievably. The levy becomes payable in full, often as a surcharge, with no way back. So commencement isn't just about keeping the permission alive. It's a moment with financial consequences, and they must be handled before the first spade goes in — not after.

title: Common misconceptions
paras: Let's turn to some common misconceptions. A handful of confident errors recur so often that they're worth naming and correcting directly.

The first: permitted development doesn't need planning permission. It is planning permission — granted in advance by the Order. Exceed a limitation, and the grant fails entirely, and the whole development is unauthorised.

The second: it's only temporary, so it's fine. Temporariness is not an exemption. A material change of use, or an operation, can be development whether or not it's meant to be permanent. And, we'll take it down eventually, is not a substitute for permission.

The third: it's inside my boundary, so it's my business. Planning control is not about ownership. What you do on your own land is development or it is not, regardless of where the red line runs. Your boundary confers no immunity.

The fourth: building regulations approval means the planning side is sorted. They are separate regimes doing separate jobs. Building regulations are about the safety and construction of the building. Planning is about the use and development of land. Approval under one says nothing about the other.

The fifth: it's been there for years, so it must be lawful. Perhaps — but only if the time for enforcement has actually passed. The periods are specific, and the evidence must prove it. Age alone does not confer lawfulness. The immunity rules do, and they have their own tests and their own timescales.

And the sixth: we dug a trench, so we've commenced. Only if the works were lawful — meaning in accordance with the permission and its pre-commencement conditions. Dig the trench in breach of a condition precedent and, on the Whitley principle, you may have commenced nothing at all.

title: The sequence as a discipline
paras: The sequence as a discipline. Return, at the end, to the beginning. The whole of this essay reduces to three questions taken in strict order — and the argument is that the order is the method.

Is it development? Apply section 55. Remember the two limbs. Check the section 55, subsection 2, exclusions. If it isn't development, the system doesn't reach it, and you can stop. If it is, ask the second question. Where does permission come from? There are only two sources — the automatic grant of the Order, and an express grant on an application. So look to the Order first, and to an application only if the Order doesn't reach. And remember that permitted development is a real permission, with real limits, real conditions, and a real habitats trap inside it. Then, and only then, ask the third question. Has development lawfully begun? Know what section 56 requires. Discharge the pre-commencement conditions before you start. Get the approvals in writing. Record the works. And handle the levy before the first spade — because a permission that's never lawfully implemented is worth nothing at all.

None of this is difficult. That's rather the point. The expensive mistakes in planning are almost never made in the refinements — in the fine reading of a policy, or the nuance of a balance. They're made at first principles. By the person who never asked whether it was development. Who assumed the Order covered them without checking. Who dug a trench in breach of a condition, and lost a permission worth a fortune. Get the first principles right, in order, every time, and the refinements will look after themselves. Get them wrong, and no amount of cleverness further down the line will save you.

A word, before we close, on the statutory references. The Town and Country Planning Act 1990. Section 55, the meaning of development — including the section 55, subsection 1A, partial definition of building operations; the section 55, subsection 2, exclusions; and the section 55, subsection 3 a, rule on subdivision of a dwellinghouse. Section 56, the time when development is begun — including the section 56, subsection 4, definition of material operation. Section 57, planning permission required for development. And sections 191 and 192, certificates of lawfulness of existing, and of proposed, use or development.

Then the secondary legislation. The Town and Country Planning Use Classes Order 1987, as amended — the use classes, and changes within a class. The Town and Country Planning, General Permitted Development, England, Order 2015, as amended — article 3, granting permission for the classes in Schedule 2. And the Conservation of Habitats and Species Regulations 2017, regulations 75 to 78 — approval of permitted development likely to affect a European site.

And the cases. Whitley and Sons against the Secretary of State for Wales, in 1992 — works in breach of a condition are not carried out under the permission. And Pioneer Aggregates U-K Limited against the Secretary of State for the Environment, in 1985 — a planning permission cannot be abandoned.
All of this is England only, and you should always check the legislation as it stands today, on legislation dot gov dot U-K. This is general guidance — not advice on any specific site or proposal.
